text1: A gunship retrofitted by integrating modular cargo components and modified Mudskipper modules onto a Gemini-class hull. The most notable tradeoff is its fragile shields. Despite structural weaknesses, its overwhelming firepower and ease of modification earn it high praise—among factions capable of utilizing it.

A battlecruiser designed to address the Invincible Fleet's lack of mobility, albeit with slightly reduced firepower. Compared to the Onslaught-class, it boasts superior maneuverability and highly customizable weapon mounts.

While the Domain-era Invincible Fleet left its mark on history (and the Persean Sector) with the Onslaught and Legion-class fortresses, they struggled to fulfill all roles efficiently. The Alice-class bridges the gap between battleships and cruisers, retaining heavy firepower and armor while gaining independent operational capabilities through enhanced mobility and a versatile weapon system.

Though overshadowed by the Onslaught and other capital ships, this vessel's adaptability secures its place in this fractured era.

A battlecruiser designed to address the Invincible Fleet's lack of mobility, albeit with slightly reduced firepower. Compared to the Onslaught-class, it boasts superior maneuverability and highly customizable weapon mounts. A minor flight deck was added to compensate for its weaker firepower, though system compatibility issues reduced its performance.

While the Domain-era Invincible Fleet left its mark on history (and the Persean Sector) with the Onslaught and Legion-class fortresses, they struggled to fulfill all roles efficiently. The Alice-class bridges the gap between battleships and cruisers, retaining heavy firepower and armor while gaining independent operational capabilities through enhanced mobility and a versatile weapon system.

Though overshadowed by the Onslaught and other capital ships, this vessel's adaptability secures its place in this fractured era.

A battlecruiser designed to address the Invincible Fleet's lack of mobility, albeit with slightly reduced firepower. Compared to the Onslaught-class, it boasts superior maneuverability and highly customizable weapon mounts. A large ballistic mount was added to compensate for its weaker firepower, though system compatibility issues reduced its performance.

While the Domain-era Invincible Fleet left its mark on history (and the Persean Sector) with the Onslaught and Legion-class fortresses, they struggled to fulfill all roles efficiently. The Alice-class bridges the gap between battleships and cruisers, retaining heavy firepower and armor while gaining independent operational capabilities through enhanced mobility and a versatile weapon system.

Though overshadowed by the Onslaught and other capital ships, this vessel's adaptability secures its place in this fractured era.

An Expansion Era carrier battleship: fast, agile, and armed with devastating firepower.

Powered by advanced engines, this massive hull achieves remarkable speed. Equipped with advanced targeting systems and four standard flight decks, it threatens all foes.

As the high-tech successor to the Legion-class carrier battleship, the Astle stands as another Domain marvel—enduring pressure from specialized ships like the Paragon and Astral, or comparisons to the Odyssey-class. Yet its versatility and raw power ensure dominance in any scenario.

A hybrid hull cobbled from Kite, Hound, and Sunder parts, prioritizing speed and missile armament.

Criminals covet this retrofit for hit-and-run raids. Its blistering speed and burst firepower allow swift destruction of merchant convoys or suicidal charges against hardened targets. Simply put, this Sunder variant outpaces fighters and leaves most warships in the dust.

A hybrid hull cobbled from Kite, Hound, and Sunder parts, prioritizing speed and missile armament.

Criminals covet this retrofit for hit-and-run raids. Its blistering speed and burst firepower allow swift destruction of merchant convoys or suicidal charges against hardened targets. Simply put, this Sunder variant outpaces fighters and leaves most warships in the dust.

A hybrid hull cobbled from Kite, Hound, and Sunder parts, prioritizing speed and ballistic armament.

Criminals covet this retrofit for hit-and-run raids. Its blistering speed and burst firepower allow swift destruction of merchant convoys or suicidal charges against hardened targets. Simply put, this Sunder variant outpaces fighters and leaves most warships in the dust.

Luddic Path zealots repurposed a Prometheus fuel hauler—loaded with "Hellfire" (antimatter fuel)—into a mobile crusade platform.

The gutted Prometheus was stuffed with nano-forges to mass-produce fighters. However, the sheer number of flight decks destabilizes its systems, jeopardizing the ship itself.

A superheavy frigate armed with two Terminator drone wings—its firepower rivals destroyers.

The Molly-class drone carrier emerged pre-Collapse as a notorious budget-padding project. Borrowing Tempest-class tech, its symmetrical hull sacrificed medium missile mounts for stability. Conservative design pleased Domain Navy brass, as experimental ships often incurred excessive upkeep.

Note: Its excessive armament strains the flux core.

A high-speed battleship integrating mobile firepower and heavy armor, believed to be comprehensively improved using legacy technology from Mithril and the Onslaught. The Dreamer-class is a decisive force in the Dominant Era grand fleets, nearly omnipotent, capable of regular line-of-battle duels and defensive operations, as well as initiating single-point assaults to break enemy lines.

From any perspective, the Dreamer-class is an over-engineered design, whether in its oversized hull or its massive number of weapon mounts. This show-off design originated from an advanced ship design project in the golden age of the Domain—intended to display the Domain's formidable technical prowess and industrial capacity, deterring numerous anti-Domain independent organizations. Behind the Dreamer-class were the most authoritative Dominant Era engineers from the Orion Shipyards, joined by fresh blood familiar with Core Era doctrines. They ignored the production bottlenecks of the vessel, acting solely to launch a massive ship symbolizing the entire Dominant Era, and declaring to the sector: 'The era of heavy armor, ballistics, and thermal pulse cannons will never end.'

As a versatile capital ship, the Dreamer-class remains extremely difficult to mass-produce, though several refits before the Collapse achieved spare parts interchangeability with other classic Dominant Era designs like the Onslaught-class. Historical records generally agree that this class never reached remote, undeveloped regions like the Persean Sector. Therefore, the sector's current factions closely monitor any sightings of this type of warship, as any rogue group possessing such a refit would be a threat that the fragile colonies in remote star systems could hardly withstand.

A heavily armed freighter famed for versatility and ballistic arrays.

By merchant standards, the Rosebush is overarmed—accelerated autoloaders and three integrated Mining Hornet wings belong on warships. Initially military-grade, civilian refits replaced its drones but retained enough firepower to duel small combatants.

This reliable freighter is favored by cash-strapped or risk-prone captains. Pirates increasingly deploy it in large-scale raids.

A carrier battleship rebuilt from Hegemony blueprints lost during the AI Wars.

Tri-Tachyon’s leaked blueprints inspired pirates to combine Legion-class hulls with Onslaught-class Thermal Pulse Cannons. The Ghoul-class pairs these cannons with composite mounts and three flight decks, overwhelming colony defense fleets.

Under seasoned pirate captains, this resurrected terror haunts underdefended worlds. As pirates say: "The time to feast on the Domain’s corpse is now!"

A battleship focused on long-range suppression. Its limited turrets resemble heavy cruisers, but its Weapon Overdrive system enables devastating salvos.

Poor at close combat, it often serves as a command center or fire support vessel for smaller fleets.

A fragile armed freighter by Expansion Era standards.

Despite its lavish appearance, the Larks-class cuts costs aggressively to serve as a budget light freighter. Its strong shield is hampered by civilian-grade power grids, leaving only minimal redundancy for mining lasers and PD. Survival relies on heavy PD lasers, flare launchers, and decent mobility.

Affordable and reliable, it handles minor pirate threats like shuttle-borne raiders. Its speed also suits emergency logistics or military auxiliary roles.

A Core Era carrier famed for its numerous flight decks.

The Heron’s bulkier cousin, designed by Altair Tech. Unlike the Heron’s focus on mobility, the Forsteri prioritizes fleet-wide carrier support. Its rounded hull (earning the "Emperor Penguin" nickname) houses five flight decks and two large ballistic turrets for suppression.

Widely used by the Persean League for its efficient strike craft deployment.

A light destroyer built for firepower. The Henchman’s integrated Weapon Overload Reload system enhances ballistic range and output.

Colonies facing raids deploy it as rapid-response heavy firepower, leaving pirates and Luddic saboteurs deeply impressed.

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

A swift, elegant, and lethally armed cruiser threatening even capital ships.

Named after a docile Earth bird, the Nipponia-class is anything but gentle. Critics initially dismissed its oversized hull, but combat silenced them. A prow-mounted large energy mount and powerful engines let it strike safely from range.

Proudly serving Hegemony/League fleets, this Eagle-class sibling is a force to reckon with.

A swift, elegant, and lethally armed cruiser threatening even capital ships.

Named after a docile Earth bird, the Nipponia-class is anything but gentle. Critics initially dismissed its oversized hull, but combat silenced them. A prow-mounted large energy mount and powerful engines let it strike safely from range.

The Mk2 variant sacrifices missile racks for flight decks to counter small craft. Exclusively serving the Hegemony.

A Domain-era cruiser prioritizing armor over firepower. Compact and heavily armored, it’s common in colonial patrols.

Colonial militias favor its defense over offensive upgrades—sufficient for pirate freighters. This reliable design thrives in the Persean Sector.

A fortress ship rivaling stations. Its purpose remains unknown.

The Holy See combines massive hulls, overwhelming firepower, and logistics for small colonies. Automated systems are efficient, but crew and resource demands hinder deep-space operations. Perhaps a technician’s fantasy or a warlord’s last stand.

This monolithic fortress uses an integrated design.

A fortress ship rivaling stations. Its purpose remains unknown.

The Holy See combines massive hulls, overwhelming firepower, and logistics for small colonies. Automated systems are efficient, but crew and resource demands hinder deep-space operations. Perhaps a technician’s fantasy or a warlord’s last stand.

This monolithic fortress uses a modular design.

A small cruiser with suspiciously oversized components.

The Harasser’s cramped layout, exposed engines, and flight decks draw scrutiny. Some speculate it’s a detachable module from a larger vessel or a Domain-era "raider design." Post-Collapse, such theories are unverifiable.

Lacking missiles for quick kills, it remains a credible threat.

A Core Era micro-carrier for harassment. The Whitebird-C’s oversized decks strain its fragile flux core, often limiting pilots to standard operations.

Cheap, fast, and flimsy—even mining crews consider using this military-grade carrier.

A Core Era heavy frigate for harassment. The Whitebird-S’s firepower surpasses the Hammerhead, but its power grid forces downgraded weapons.

Cheap yet sturdy, it excels in fire support roles.

An Expansion Era heavy cruiser renowned for firepower and shields. Its fortress-like shields withstand capital-grade fire, while High-Energy Focus and energy weapons punish enemies daring to enter its range. Though outshone by the Aurora’s advanced design, the rugged Demolisher held its own in Domain fleets.

A testament to blending old doctrines with new tech.

A highly mobile Domain-era battlecruiser. Once admired for speed, shields, and elegance, post-Collapse obsolescence limits it to poor colonies, explorers, or speculators hoping to revive it.

This beautiful war machine sleeps—awaiting a museum or rebirth. Even outdated, it fights valiantly.

A highly mobile Domain-era battlecruiser. Post-Collapse upgrades added four flight decks, letting it project strike craft from its battlecruiser frame.

This revived machine now stalks the battlefield like an archer.

A highly mobile Domain-era battlecruiser. Post-Collapse radical refits transformed it into an asymmetric monster. The Mk3 wields Tyrfing-class "Flashfire" rapid-fire systems to obliterate peers.

Reborn as a knight, it holds the line.

A sturdy, well-armed light cruiser. Post-Collapse colonies built it using Enterprise-class blueprints, offering early defense. Pragmatic design crams flight decks and diverse mounts into a compact hull.

Now often deployed for long-range support.
A fast destroyer shredding small craft with ballistic spam. Useless against capitals, yet overeager captains charge them anyway. At least retreat is quick—explaining why tavern tales persist.

A mobile cruiser militarized from the Venture-class. Stripped of survey gear and hyper-optimized for speed. Efficient mounts and integrated flight decks eliminate carrier dependence. It charges like fearless cavalry.

Sleek yet deadly, it anchors battlelines alone.

A fast, defensive Expansion Era armed freighter. Designed to resupply Domain task forces in remote sectors or support invasions.

Post-Collapse, Tri-Tachyon retrofitted it with salvage rigs for practicality.

A retro-style fast cruiser emphasizing forward firepower at the cost of rear defense. Lacks tactical systems for chasing foes. Combines light and heavy mounts to threaten all targets.

Mainly used by the Luddic Church—if you see one, expect a devout knight.

A Core Era carrier famed for flight decks. The Sower balances six decks with mobility and power grids, outperforming the Astral in versatility.

Widely used by the Persean League.

A rare fast battleship blending capital-grade defenses, mobility, and firepower. The Vidofnir-class, designed by "Mbayé-Gogol" to compete with the Conquest, emphasizes energy weapons. Domain Navy officials favored the Conquest, relegating the Vidofnir to ceremonial roles or elite guards.

Post-Collapse, its hidden potential shines—fighting Hegemony fleets in the AI Wars and raiding convoys.

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon now fears this ship’s disruptive potential—hiding fangs or cowering in fear?

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon now fears this ship’s disruptive potential—hiding fangs or cowering in fear?

A short-range "armed" freighter beloved by remote haulers. Cheap, fast, efficient—and fragile. Despite downsizing from the Hound-class, its cargo capacity remains decent, though hyperspace fuel costs are disproportionate.

Post-Collapse, such crude designs proliferate—a testament to the Sector’s decline.

A multirole logistics frigate. "Prospecting, scanning, salvaging! Spacious holds, crew quarters, fuel tanks! Defense, speed, and mining drones! All in the Tracker! Ditch your relics for the future!"—so claimed its ads. But high upkeep and undersized gear deterred users. Competing with Shepherd-class and Ventures, it faded into obscurity.

Still, its versatility suits short-haul missions. Scholars with technical skills might appreciate its built-in gear.

An Expansion Era destroyer with absurd firepower. Flexible energy and missile mounts let it strike like a viper.

Though flux-limited, it annihilates targets efficiently.

A post-Collapse multirole fuel hauler. Praised by some governments as "innovative," but despised by crews and pirates alike. Crews hate hauling antimatter in its fragile hull, cramped corridors, and useless guns. Admirals scoff at its mediocre capacity. Pirates avoid this volatile "firework."

Deucalion crews inherit the Atlas-class’s misfortune—magnified.

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

An eerie flame-like glow emanates from its hull...

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

"The Persean Sector’s twilight—or dawn? While these drones roam, no sunrise shall come."—Hegemony propaganda from the First AI War.

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

"Tri-Tachyon’s automatons harass our allies. Our forces cannot attack and defend simultaneously."—First AI War field report.

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

An autonomous battlecruiser equipped with unbelievable teleportation capabilities, leaning more towards crushing lighter vessels compared to the Radiant. Ironically, after the First AI War, the Hegemony's 'Victory Declaration' explicitly claimed that all autonomous fleet strategic bases had been completely destroyed. Yet, as more and more autonomous warships traverse remote systems with impunity, people have realized that it was merely self-deception.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon, who created it, is unwilling to let this ship tear apart the existing peace. Is this an attempt to hide their fangs, or is it merely out of fear?

A fast destroyer specializing in harassing capitals. Standard guns lack punch, but flexible mounts allow heavy torpedoes or EMP ion weapons.

A standard flight deck aids scouting and disruption.

A raider playing vanguard in small fleets. Fragile but threatening with missiles and speed. Similar to retrofitted Kites—hit hard, then flee. Underrated during the Domain’s dominance, its modern resurgence aids pirates and Tri-Tachyon guerrillas.

The Hegemony now acknowledges fast ships’ importance, resurrecting this design.

An old-gen superheavy cruiser-carrier hybrid. Unlike the civilian Mora, the Aphrodite emphasizes direct firepower alongside flight decks. Low build costs masked by high upkeep—manageable under Domain logistics, but post-Collapse, few factions bother.

A militarized freighter beloved by haulers, pirates, and bounty hunters. Originally a planetary bomber, its excessive armament lets it brawl with warships—shieldless.

Common in pirate raids—and patrols hunting those same pirates.

A short-range "armed" freighter beloved by remote haulers. Cheap, fast, efficient—and fragile. Double the Puppy-class’s size but equally weak defenses.

Post-Collapse, such crude designs proliferate—a testament to the Sector’s decline.
A commemorative cruiser-sized yacht with archaic luxury. Based on an Old Earth film, its name and design reference a fictional "flying boat." Rare and sought-after by blueprint hunters.

A large, lightly armored warship embodying the Domain Navy’s pursuit of firepower and mobility. Its long-range guns and speed let commanders deploy it strategically.

Dubbed "vulnerable" compared to "invincible" designs, it was relegated to secondary roles.

A ship radiating extremism. The Domain Navy deemed it irresponsible—optimized to annihilate cruisers but helpless against capitals. Reviving the "pocket battleship" concept, it was reclassified as a battlecruiser during the Second AI War.

Tri-Tachyon used it to ravage Hegemony convoys and patrols. Losses? Just cheap mercenary lives.

An Expansion Era destroyer designed by Domain Naval R&D (with Tri-Tachyon influence). Based on the Hyperion, its stabilized "Phantom Jump" system reduces maintenance and enables larger ship teleportation.

Tri-Tachyon stole blueprints pre-Second AI War, deploying it to cripple Hegemony logistics.

A Core Era redesign of the Mystia-class. Domain traditionalists demanded a slow, imposing battleship for show. Retaining long-range guns and ditching missiles, it gained large energy mounts and reduced fighter dependency.

During the First AI War, Ziz-class lines defended colonies effectively before Tri-Tachyon reinforcements arrived.

A remnant of Tri-Tachyon’s AI fleets. Officially banned by the Domain (and Hegemony) and despised by the Luddic Church, these ships were supposedly purged during the First AI War—or so history claims.

This unmanned battleship emphasizes armor and mobility over offense. The Hegemony’s post-war "Victory Declaration" insisted all AI strongholds were destroyed. Yet their resurgence in remote systems exposes this as delusion.

An eerie flame-like glow emanates from its hull...

A post-Collapse super-freighter retrofit. Reinforced Atlas hulls with external bracing become ad-hoc artillery platforms.

Designed for bulk cargo delivery to remote outposts, its size and slowness attract pirates and fanatics. Hegemony uses it for seasonal agriculture hauls, guarded by cruiser fleets.

Commanding one is punishment for competence—tedium punctuated by terror.

A Domain Navy flagship designed as a fleet centerpiece and tech testbed. Initially an all-big-gun dreadnought, later modified. Equipped with "Flashfire" ballistic accelerators for devastating salvos—or "drawing the cursed blade."

Post-Collapse, it passed through mad owners seeking glory, each meeting ruin.

A durable light destroyer with archaic ablative foam armor. Weak weapons, but engineers later added auxiliary systems to boost output.

The Hegemony traded its blueprints to the Luddic Church during the Second AI War for covert support.

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

An autonomous battleship boasting heavy armor and array mounts, leaning more towards a defensive role compared to the Radiant. Ironically, after the First AI War, the Hegemony's 'Victory Declaration' explicitly claimed that all autonomous fleet bases had been completely destroyed. Yet, as more autonomous warships roam remote systems, people have come to realize it was nothing but empty propaganda.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon, who created it, is unwilling to let this ship tear apart the existing peace. Is this an attempt to hide their fangs, or is it merely out of fear?

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

An autonomous carrier with a terrifying number of flight decks and a prototype recall device, leaning more towards defense compared to the Radiant. Ironically, after the First AI War, the Hegemony's 'Victory Declaration' claimed that all autonomous fleet bases had been completely wiped out. Yet, as more automated warships roam the fringes, people have realized that it was merely self-deceptive propaganda.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon, who created it, is unwilling to let this ship tear apart the existing peace. Is this an attempt to hide their fangs, or is it merely out of fear?

A variant of the Conquest-class, featuring weapon configurations, hullmods, and tactical systems modified to focus on missile strikes, along with slightly increased speed. Otherwise, not much has changed.

A small carrier following the concept of the Mora-class. It has only one flight deck, but offers decent defenses, making it more suited for assault operations than typical carriers.

The Flingstone-class is a post-Collapse ship, designed by an engineer on Nova Maxios. During a resource supply cutoff caused by pirate raids, he designed a small carrier to provide aviation support for the orbital station. He personally led a small fleet built around these carriers to harass pirate fleets near the jump point, buying enough time for a merchant convoy preparing to break through. Because the Flingstone-class possesses reliability and resilience remarkable even among frigates, it quickly became popular among independent operators and was adopted to some extent by Hegemony patrol fleets.

The Flingstone-class is neither fast nor equipped with a powerful power grid, but its hull durability is noticeably higher than other frigates, and it features a damper field. This micro-carrier can deploy standard fighters to support smaller ships, or carry support fighters like the Hoplon-class as 'external weapon mounts' for close-quarters combat.

A fleet carrier converted from the Prometheus-class. The modification simply consists of roughly integrating a nanoforge inside the fuel tanks and configuring some standard decks, resulting in low maintenance efficiency. It is mostly used by non-faction groups as an aviation vessel for small fleets.

While the notorious Luddic Path variant of the Prometheus-class is well known, this carrier conversion takes a completely different path. The designer of this refit was an engineer from Asharu who initially designed logistics equipment for the Hegemony auxiliary force, and now designs civilian refitted ships for a deep-space exploration conglomerate. The Prometheus (CV) was his answer to the pirates' Colossus MK3. Later, a pirate raid from Qaras benefited this refitted ship, and the conglomerate began promoting it as a commercial product.

We cannot say for sure whether this ghastly heap, as nauseating as the Ghoul-class, counts as a 'Prometheus MK3', but we can tell at a glance that it is yet another holy war weapon or spiritual totem of Luddic Path zealots. The parts for this ship come from extremely diverse sources, and none of the subsystems run in harmony, but this does not prevent it from becoming the strongest war machine possessed by the Path.

The Iron Dome-class is a response to the Aurora-class cruiser, designed by the Orion Shipyards during the era when Expansion Era ships dominated. This heavy ship features high-power engines capable of keeping up with Expansion Era vessels, armaments stronger than its predecessor, the Eradicator-class, and is fitted with a heavy flux capacitor completely atypical of Dominant Era ships—all supporting it in rushing to the front lines for close-quarters combat.

The Iron Dome-class entered the Domain stage during an era when Tri-Tachyon and Domain naval contractors were working closely. At that time, fast warships carrying heavy energy weapons were every captain's favorite, whereas Dominant Era ships either couldn't keep up with these fast vessels (like the Dominator-class) or sacrificed too much for speed (like the Eradicator-class). During its development, several key designers left due to pessimism regarding the prospects of Domain ships. After the Collapse, many captains similarly neglected this high-speed cruiser, believing that even with optimization, it couldn't match the Aurora-class. Ultimately, the Iron Dome-class found its way to the captains of the Luddic Church, and these devout, straightforward paladins—with their unique insights into safety override systems—became the turning point of its fate.

Although its equivalent power grid cannot match other heavy cruisers, the Iron Dome-class can still use a small missile array inherited from the Retribution-class, paired with three ballistic mounts of different sizes and a strong drive system to deliver fierce blows. In the words of the Luddites, the Iron Dome is 'born without fear, carrying its scars.' When Moloch and Mammon laugh at outdated Dominant Era technology, the heavy blade of the Iron Dome will descend from the heavens, severing these demons with courage that transcends life and death.

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

We must admit that the optimization speed of the Remnant's fleet doctrine is much faster than expected, as the first appearance of the Thunderbolt-class was only 15 days after the first appearance of the Radiant-class. This hull, the size of a battlecruiser, is somehow configured with subsystems of a standard cruiser—suspected to be the result of the Nexus trying to bypass Domain standard safety protocols. Its behavioral patterns are very similar to its counterpart, the Nova-class, both possessing strong hostility toward light ships, making it a huge reinforcement for assault-oriented Remnant fleets.

Its super-heavy hull makes the Thunderbolt-class the most expensive cruiser to deploy in the sector. Compared to the Nova-class, its protection is slightly inferior, but its maneuverability is even greater. The Thunderbolt-class is equipped with an intimidating heavy missile array and uses an enhanced loader. Guided by AI algorithms that have no concept of death, this heavy warship will become one of the most threatening drone ships in the sector.

We still do not know how the Luddic Path found an Apostle of Sin heavy cannon, but it is now mounted on this Colossus-class. Although the freighter hull means its survivability is still worrying, its firepower can indeed threaten many capital ships.

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

An autonomous gunboat boasting heavy frontal firepower, leaning more towards a defensive role compared to the Radiant. Ironically, after the First AI War, the Hegemony's 'Victory Declaration' explicitly claimed that all autonomous fleet strategic bases had been completely destroyed. Yet, as more and more autonomous warships traverse remote systems with impunity, people have realized that it was merely self-deception.

Ironically, Tri-Tachyon, who created it, is unwilling to let this ship tear apart the existing peace. Is this an attempt to hide their fangs, or is it merely out of fear?

A few Harasser-class ships were unfortunate enough to be disabled under the onslaught of a pack of wolves, only to find appreciation under a crude new owner. A massive missile mount stripped from some unknown vessel now occupies the entire hangar space, and two large ballistic mounts have been violently modified to be compatible with missile weapons. The end result—rather than 'harassing', this pirate ship is built to 'crush'.

'Utilitarian thinking will change everything,' and the Flame Sparrow-class is the symbol of this phrase. This is a super destroyer with pathologically terrifying firepower, considered in some sense a 'Super Brawler TT', and its versatility is wider than you might think.

The Flame Sparrow-class is a ship developed by Tri-Tachyon after the First AI War, built specifically for Tri-Tachyon skirmish fleets, with only a few mercenaries and agents able to acquire this unprecedentedly powerful destroyer. Unlike the Hyperion-class, which was criticized as a 'pocket princess', the Flame Sparrow-class is more logistically friendly and possesses higher burst potential. Its deterrence in small fleets is almost equivalent to a battlecruiser—provided that its conventional shield capacity is not overloaded with flux.

Interestingly, the design team of the Flame Sparrow-class were fanatical supporters of the Brawler TT-class. This team frequently argued with other directors' design teams over the practicality of ships like the Tempest and Omen, and the Flame Sparrow-class was likely a 'spite' product born from one of those arguments...

A legacy of the AI fleets developed by Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Strictly banned by the Domain (and subsequently the Hegemony), and deeply despised by the Church of Galactic Redemption at the time, the First AI War witnessed the complete purging of autonomous warships from the sector. At least, that is how official history records it.

The Photonic Arc-class is a destroyer that perfectly fits the fearsome combat nature of artificial intelligence. It features mounts that rival most destroyers and even a few cruisers, along with excellent straight-line speed. En masse, Photonic Arc-class destroyers are typically more fearsome than a similar-sized Brilliant-class, as most crewed ships cannot protect themselves against the onslaught of such autonomous wolfpacks.

Interestingly, like the Glimmer-class, the Photonic Arc-class was designed and mass-produced by the Remnant Nexus during the Second AI War, bearing a similarly strong exotic style... as if we have seen this design somewhere before.
A modified ship of unknown origin. The hull design details are similar to many entries strictly regulated by COMSEC.

On the surface, this warship is merely a variant of a classic Remnant design, but the integrated crew quarters tell you it is not that simple. This ship is managed by a replica of an instance named 'Alice', and its performance parameters have been adjusted to Core Era standards for a single purpose: serving in any future conflict that might occur.

The Glimmer (AC)-class is the smallest of the vessels managed by Alice. It is an integration of the Centurion and Glimmer classes, losing small synergy mounts in exchange for upgrades to other mounts and high-level protection. Obviously, this prototype design has various deficiencies, but if used correctly, it remains a qualified line frigate.

"...We had no choice but to take the initiative, using these invaders' hulls as a means of counterattack. 'Alice' tends to give these ships higher survivability and long-range firepower to allow us to avoid unfavorable battles while launching effective counter-strikes, hoping it can bring some color of hope to our bleak prospects." — Recovered data fragment from planet Ces Cyclops

On the surface, this warship is merely a variant of a classic Remnant design, but the integrated crew quarters tell you it is not that simple. This ship is managed by a replica of an instance named 'Alice', and its performance parameters have been adjusted to Core Era standards for a single purpose: serving in any future conflict that might occur.

The Lumen (AC)-class is the second ship class controlled by the Alice instance. Unlike the strike-focused prototype, it features hybrid weapon mounts similar to a cruiser's and enhanced engines, along with a sturdier hull. The price paid is a relatively high deployment cost for a destroyer and a weaker shield. This destroyer is best suited for engaging same-class or smaller vessels from a safe distance, while its powerful engines allow it to maneuver away when larger ships approach.

"...The results are pleasant. In theory, this hull can possess firepower close to a cruiser. We are currently trying to find component expansion methods that do not affect engine performance. I know this keeps the deployment cost of this variant high, but we absolutely need a sufficiently reliable light warship." — Recovered data fragment from planet Ces Cyclops

On the surface, this warship is merely a variant of a classic Remnant design, but the integrated crew quarters tell you it is not that simple. This ship is managed by a replica of an instance named 'Alice', and its performance parameters have been adjusted to Core Era standards for a single purpose: serving in any future conflict that might occur.

The Brilliant (AC)-class is the star of the Alice Protocol series. Unlike the prototype, which is constrained by fitting limitations and shipsystem issues, this refitted cruiser boasts a heavy weapon array terrifying to the whole sector, alongside mobility that most heavy cruisers could only dream of. Without a doubt, this heavy cruiser is the backbone of the series, possessing incomparable tactical value.

"...RDDUS-Daedalus will make contact with target Gulf as planned. The support fleet is silent in the ring of the fourth quadrant... We have never had such a moment of joy. This battle will determine whether we can counterattack the target system and reach the place where the invaders are." — Recovered data fragment from planet Ces Cyclops

On the surface, this warship is merely a variant of a classic Remnant design, but the integrated crew quarters tell you it is not that simple. This ship is managed by a replica of an instance named 'Alice', and its performance parameters have been adjusted to Core Era standards for a single purpose: serving in any future conflict that might occur.

The Scintilla (AC)-class has the highest survivability among its class in the Alice Protocol series. Its appearance is surprisingly unmodified, but it has received complete upgrades to its weapon mounts and armor plating, and is fitted with the same damper field generator as the Glimmer (AC). This heavy cruiser possesses top-tier pressure-absorption capability and all-range heavy firepower comparable to classic assault cruisers. Although it lacks the mobility and strike capability of the Brilliant (AC), its existence likely indicates a revision of its creator's fleet doctrine.

"...I must admit that we are 'back to square one'. The invaders' capital-grade units have completely blocked our hopes of breaking into the target system again. The hyperspace defense battle is replayed, and we must stall the enemy strike fleet, even if they are endless." — Recovered data fragment from planet Ces Cyclops

On the surface, this warship is merely a variant of a classic Remnant design, but the integrated crew quarters tell you it is not that simple. This ship is managed by a replica of an instance named 'Alice', and its performance parameters have been adjusted to Core Era standards for a single purpose: serving in any future conflict that might occur.

The Pulse (AC)-class is the largest warship Alice can control, at least for now. This capital ship appears to be a fusion of two different philosophies of 'heavy ship countering wolfpacks.' It possesses exaggerated speed for its class, alongside numerous mounts compatible with missile weapons and pre-installed expanded racks. The trade-off is that its paper survivability remains at the level of the Onslaught-class, which is mismatching with its Paragon-grade deployment cost. When you need to deploy this capital ship, it generally indicates that the pressure you face from smaller vessels has reached a high level.

"The invaders' fleet is gradually approaching our jump point, yet research on them remains shrouded in mystery. 'Alice' is constantly repairing the data core of the target capital ship, bringing bad news every time: if these are border rebels that could be driven back by pioneers, why are their automated mechanisms so advanced? If this is truly an enemy of the Domain, why can 'Alice' find Tri-Tachyon trademarks in the data core of their capital ships?" — Recovered data fragment from planet Ces Cyclops

A warship built upon the design of the Hegemony's founders—the Domain's 14th Heavy Battlegroup. The series of radical structural modifications using pre-Collapse industrial technology makes it a primary subject for studying the Domain Navy's 'decisive battle' doctrine.

Although classified as a high-speed cruiser, the lack of a highly mobile tactical system makes it difficult to pursue fast targets. The design team of the Punch-class chose a combination of numerous small weapons and a single heavy weapon, allowing it to deliver effective strikes against both light and heavy units.

The Punch-class, as a somewhat 'obsolete' creation, was primarily used by the Luddic Church, whose industrial capacity is quite weak. If you find your companion owning this ship, he is highly likely a pious and brave Luddic knight. However, the First AI War triggered a series of technical exchanges between the Hegemony and the Church. A portion of the Punch-class underwent special modifications, appearing as they do now with new weapons. Although highly likely inferior to the original 14th Battlegroup vessels, it is better than nothing.

The Dreamer-class is already rare, and this prototype originating from the 14th Battlegroup is exceptionally scarce. The Dreamer (XIV)-class stands as a true fleet emperor in the 'decisive battle doctrine' doctrine, boasting even more exaggerated performance compared to its standard version. It remains unknown whether this ship is a rare flagship from before the First AI War, or a vessel constructed by unknown entities after the Collapse.

A high-speed battleship integrating mobile firepower and heavy armor, believed to be comprehensively improved using legacy technology from Mithril and the Onslaught. The Dreamer-class is a decisive force in the Dominant Era grand fleets, nearly omnipotent, capable of regular line-of-battle duels and defensive operations, as well as initiating single-point assaults to break enemy lines.

From any perspective, the Dreamer-class is an over-engineered design, whether in its oversized hull or its massive number of weapon mounts. This show-off design originated from an advanced ship design project in the golden age of the Domain—intended to display the Domain's formidable technical prowess and industrial capacity, deterring numerous anti-Domain independent organizations. Behind the Dreamer-class were the most authoritative Dominant Era engineers from the Orion Shipyards, joined by fresh blood familiar with Core Era doctrines. They ignored the production bottlenecks of the vessel, acting solely to launch a massive ship symbolizing the entire Dominant Era, and declaring to the sector: 'The era of heavy armor, ballistics, and thermal pulse cannons will never end.'

As a versatile capital ship, the Dreamer-class remains extremely difficult to mass-produce, though several refits before the Collapse achieved spare parts interchangeability with other classic Dominant Era designs like the Onslaught-class. Historical records generally agree that this class never reached remote, undeveloped regions like the Persean Sector. Therefore, the sector's current factions closely monitor any sightings of this type of warship, as any rogue group possessing such a refit would be a threat that the fragile colonies in remote star systems could hardly withstand.

A warship built upon the design of the Hegemony's founders—the Domain's 14th Heavy Battlegroup. The series of radical structural modifications using pre-Collapse industrial technology makes it a primary subject for studying the Domain Navy's 'decisive battle' doctrine.

A battlecruiser designed to address the Invincible Fleet's lack of mobile forces. Completely different from its standard version, this specially customized warship is equipped with an additional pair of heavy punishers, yielding greater mobility than the Onslaught and more customizable weapon slots.

Although the early Domain Invincible Fleet left a glorious mark on history and even today's Persean Sector with the steel walls of Onslaughts and Legions, it ultimately could not perform all tasks efficiently. To fill the gap between battleships and cruisers, this ship was born, retaining a formidable weapon layout and heavy armor. Enhanced mobility and a more complete combat system give it greater capability for independent operations.

Although it has long been, and may continue to be, overshadowed by the Onslaught and other large capital battleships, this vessel's great combat versatility secures it a place in this fractured era.

A warship carrying the signature of the Derelict but with a noticeably different internal structure. Lacking a serial number, this vessel resembles Derelict automated ships, using simple, reliable systems to control complex machinery, but integrates crew quarters for a standard crew. This ship is perhaps proof of the existence of some brilliant engineers of the past.

"Ignoring scattered old forge spare parts is usually considered a wasteful act. Those remnants of industrial giants are often collected by enthusiasts, but few 'collectibles' are actually useful. The exception is this small remotely-controlled frigate built from a portion of an exploratory mothership—known as the Maxwell-class, a product of blueprint hackers with miraculous endurance."

A warship carrying the signature of the Derelict but with a noticeably different internal structure. Lacking a serial number, this vessel resembles Derelict automated ships, using simple, reliable systems to control complex machinery, but integrates crew quarters for a standard crew. This ship is perhaps proof of the existence of some brilliant engineers of the past.

"Is it possible to use station-grade sensors on a destroyer-class ship? Although incredible, it is indeed feasible. Installing the core sensor and locator components of an exploratory mothership on a framework with fewer armaments, and reinforcing it with spare parts produced by nanoforges, yields the Doppler-class."

A warship carrying the signature of the Derelict but with a noticeably different internal structure. Lacking a serial number, this vessel resembles Derelict automated ships, using simple, reliable systems to control complex machinery, but integrates crew quarters for a standard crew. This ship is perhaps proof of the existence of some brilliant engineers of the past.

"During those three months besieged in the abandoned mothership, the central nanoforge operated on a tightrope, on the verge of being detected by hyper-wave sensors. Every engineer passed the TriPad containing technical specifications, incomplete blueprints, and testaments to the next person, leaving survival resources and hopes of escape to the last few. When later generations asked me, an old explorer, how I broke through the blockade of [DATA EXPUNGED] and became a legend, I would only reply: 'It was because of the will of Ulysses,' which was both the name of this salvation ship and the name of that heroic engineering team."
A post-Second AI War drone armed with PD weapons and a high-impact cannon. Formidable but lengthy rearmament.
text2: "We are still missing a navigation beacon supporting free movement to make it perfect, but unfortunately the internal space crowded by prototype equipment no longer supports further improvements. — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: A drone lethal to small craft. High-dispersion lasers shred shields and hulls.
A shieldless fighter loaded with weapons. Durable and fast, but slow to rearm.
A heavy fighter with long-range armament.
text2: "The space planning of the finished product is excellent. It can already be called a micro-gunboat, and its output potential is even greater than some shuttles. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A mining drone modified for harsh environments.
A relatively rare strike fighter, equipped with a pair of self-defense autocannons and a pair of compromised burst-specialized pulse cannons for disabling small vessels.
text2: "The ammunition bay cannot be larger. I don't want this thing to turn into a bomber version of the Talon for projection and firepower. Even though its expected targets usually have no anti-air weapons. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: What else can we say about the Star bomber? It's simply a flying Tachyon Lance, posing a major threat to any ship.

A heavy anti-ship fighter born during the Second AI War. The Thunder Dragon-class is equipped with standard anti-ship armaments and a highly reinforced frame. Its kinetic-explosive hybrid design allows it to shred light craft and suppress large ships with weak shields. The open flux-vent design allows it to fully support its devastating weaponry, while an extra damage control system helps it adapt to the harshest environments.
text2: "The space planning of the finished product is excellent. It can already be called a micro-gunboat, and its output potential is even greater than some shuttles. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A long-range ion cannon integrated into ship hulls due to size.
text2: -"I want to figure out as soon as possible how this weapon is directly connected to the ship's reactor. Its spatial footprint is much smaller than expected, looking completely unlike a variant of the thermal pulse family. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A post-Second AI War weapon designed for Lance drones.
A large-bore short-barrel cannon stressing grids but threatening armor and shields.
text2: -"Mjolnir Cannon? This time it really looks like one. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: An ultra-light AG assault gun piercing armor with precision.
A split heavy mortar firing reduced charges—still armor-cracking.
text2: -"Just in case some ships don't have a single missile slot. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A specialized laser used in material cutting.
text2: -"Hopefully nobody thinks about stuffing this into a Wasp or some other interceptor. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A quad-linked flak cannon annihilating missiles and fighters at range.
text2: -"Large ballistic slots are no longer such precious resources, there's no harm in yielding them to more practical point defense weapons. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A long-range energy weapon limited only by magnetic constraints.
text2: -"Before seeing this, I had always questioned the raison d'être of the Scintilla-class. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: An antique PD gun designed against Claw interceptors. Also damages light armor.
text2: -"Devastator? It looks even less like it. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A weak particle accelerator with ultra-high velocity via magnetic rails.
text2: -"Ion Pulser? Doesn't look like it. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: The Constraint Driver is one of the most fearsome large energy weapons in the Persean Sector. An upscaled version of the Constraint Blaster, its larger base supports extended magnetic rails, drastically boosting projectile damage and velocity at the cost of poorer flux efficiency.
text2: -"Won't such a straightforward modification get us a lawyer's letter from Tri-Tachyon? — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: A fixed Hellbore firing reduced charges. Enhanced firing mechanisms make it devastating. Tavern rumors claim: "This gun kills anything—common or otherwise."
text2: -"A simple concept with significant results, though unfortunately it's modular. After all, there aren't many perfect high-explosive ballistic weapons in the Persean Sector. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A highly compatible weapon modeled after the proximity mine launcher, featuring the smallest base among large weapons. The unstable proton clouds fired by this energy cannon possess a highly respectable blast radius, but its effective range is even shorter than standard large energy weapons.
text2: -"We are out of options. That experimental carbon battery can only support shots of that power at most, and we have to use it to ensure we don't blow our flux. — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: A piece of mining equipment. The manual says 'Do not disassemble for ship mount', yet it paradoxically features a standard weapon mount interface. While its damage to rock strata is not exaggerated, its special warhead can dissolve substances harder than rock.
text2: -"Mining Blasters are generally used to find weak spots in asteroids and metallic surfaces, whereas the 'Big Rip' can tackle the crustal substrate of diamond planets. Though, in the end, we found that warship hulls might not even be as tough as rock strata. — Data recorded by the new Producer in the dev log"

text1: An imitation or miniature version of something that 'might be high-tech equipment'. This weapon can project a stream of cryogenic particles close to absolute zero. Since its coolant source is typical ship refrigerant, its actual damage and projectile velocity are heavily compromised.
text2: -"So the equipment sample we spent so much effort to acquire... is just this? — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A weapon sitting between the autocannon and the heavy machine gun. Its projectile damage and continuous output are terrifyingly high, but its awful spread and short range limit its effectiveness. The Retribution-class might like this thing.
text2: -"It is really extremely loud. Hopefully those customers have done proper soundproofing on their hulls. — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: An intimidatingly large anti-hull weapon capable of spitting massive amounts of molten slag. Although this 'waste' cannot threaten standard armor, the strong-interaction particles within will puncture and tear at the target's hull; it is only a matter of time before severe consequences occur.
text2: -"Okay, let's call this metal particle that can melt... weld... drill through the target in one shot 'slag'. But by the way, where did you get this industrial waste? — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and an unknown buyer"

text1: A simple upscaled product of a DEM weapon. Packaging an optimized Gazer launcher with an anti-radiation polymer shell yields this weapon, with adjustments enhancing its flight speed and reload rate.
text2: -"*(Beautiful Ancyra swear words)*, can this group of authoritative engineers not even produce a decent large missile weapon? — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: Yes, you saw that right, it fires an 'On-Slay-er'—specifically, a solid metal miniature model of an Onslaught-class battleship. No one knows what the creator was thinking when designing this, but you can throw this model hard at an enemy ship's head. I guarantee it will instantly overload any ship below a cruiser in one hit.
text2: -"So you're saying we could set up a toy assembly line to rake in high profits? — Chat logs between the new Producer and Engineer Andrew"

text1: A heavy weapon of unknown origin, similar to Tri-Tachyon products only in minor technical details. This dual-linked lance can support heavy burst weapons, rapidly disabling enemies in high-flux states, if you are willing to 'sacrifice' a large energy slot.
text2: -"We have probably unlocked the full potential of the ion beam... so our next step is to market automated repair systems? — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: An extremely miniaturized Constraint Blaster, sacrificing considerable damage and velocity, but remaining practical and resolving some energy efficiency issues.
text2: -"Is this right? A small energy weapon doing long-range sniping? — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: Don't be fooled by the stubby appearance of this gun. An upscaled version of the Heavy Mauler, the Heavy Punisher is a general-purpose high-explosive weapon manufactured by the Hegemony's military factories to address anti-armor issues for large ballistic mounts, suitable for most scenarios.

An extremely intimidating cryo-cannon. The technical details of the Narwhal cannot be described normally; it not only inflicts massive damage regardless of the target, but also has the added effect of countering heavy shields.
text2: -"New tests prove the possibility of polarity reversal in classic shield fields under sample influence. Indeed, there is no physics to speak of below absolute zero. — Chat logs between Engineer Andrew and the new Producer"

text1: Not just a simple upscaled Phase Lance—it has longer range, shorter beam duration, and deals more damage to armor.

A unique ballistic weapon. Instead of using conventional ammunition belts or loaders, it connects to a container of raw materials, 'printing' quantum projectiles in the center of the pulse rail and firing them at high speed. The instability of the projectiles makes it more effective at tearing apart ship structures.
A single-shot Tachyon Lance. The special emitter structure prevents it from generating flux, and its beam duration is drastically shortened without reducing power.

A piece of mining equipment directly connected to a high-capacity ammo forge, unable to be detached. While its damage to rock strata is not exaggerated, its special warhead can dissolve substances harder than rock.
text2: -"Mining Blasters are generally used to find weak spots in asteroids and metallic surfaces, whereas the 'Big Rip' can tackle the crustal substrate of diamond planets. Though, in the end, we found that warship hulls might not even be as tough as rock strata. — Data recorded by the new Producer in the dev log"

text1: Developer test weapon, used to test projectiles that force-reduce structural value. If you see this, please ignore it.
Temporarily operates the engines at full power, significantly increasing maneuverability and top speed.
text2: Mobility

text1: Temporarily overloads the engines, greatly increasing maneuverability and top speed. Includes 2 extra charges.
text2: Mobility

text1: Deploys heavy mines from phase space. Fewer charges due to lighter capacitors.
text2: Offense

text1: Activates backup capacitors to boost energy weapon rate of fire, slightly increasing flux.
text2: Special

text1: Teleports the ship via shallow phase interaction.
text2: Offense

text1: Disrupts engine regulators, temporarily immobilizing ships.
text2: Special

text1: A weaker, flexible "Temporal Shell" with lower flux cost.
text2: Offense

text1: Accelerates local timeflow, mildly boosting engines. Generates moderate flux.
text2: Offense

text1: Dramatically increases speed and mobility briefly.
text2: Mobility

text1: Disables most weapons. Ballistics gain +500% fire rate and -25% flux.
text2: Offense

text1: Disables most weapons. Ballistics gain +500% fire rate and -25% flux.
Overloads weapons: +25% damage and fire rate (excluding missiles). No adverse effects.
text2: Offense
text3: Increases non-missile weapon damage by 25% and non-missile rate of fire by 25% for a duration.

text1: Overloads weapons: +25% damage and fire rate (excluding missiles). No adverse effects.
text3: Increases non-missile weapon damage by 25% and non-missile rate of fire by 25% for a duration.

text1: Activates temporary hardening, causing beam weapons to inflict hard flux.
text2: Special

text1: Grants +20% weapon range to non-capital allies within 3000su.
text2: Special

text1: Teleports in a direction, relocating nearby fighters within 2000su.
text2: Special

text1: Reloads all non-missile weapons.
text2: Offense

text1:
Grants the following bonuses based on the type of weapon installed in the largest synergy/composite slot:
Ballistic: +50% non-missile weapon fire rate
Missile: +100 top speed and +50% maneuverability
Energy: +50% non-missile weapon damage
text2: Special
text3: Grants different bonus effects depending on the weapon in the largest synergy/composite slot.

text1: A classic recall device, but featuring multiple charges.
text2: Offense

text1: Provides a 40% weapon range bonus and a 2x rate of fire bonus while suppressing weapon spread; when inactive, it provides a small range bonus. The system occupies a massive amount of space, and its user's mounts are typically extremely sparse.
text2: Special
text3: Significantly increases weapon range and rate of fire for a duration, providing a minor range bonus when inactive.

text1: Scrambles the target's fire control servos and damage control systems, reducing component repair speed and weapon rate of fire by 50%.
text2: Special

text1: Sprints forward at extreme speed, during which the rate of fire and ammo recovery rate of missile weapons are massively increased. Due to the high velocity, the ship will slide forward a distance after the system ends.
text2: Offense
